text.title: Python Web Framework Antipatterns
text.summary: Idiom mixing, deprecated lifecycle hooks, misleading benchmarks, and LLM-specific failure modes.

antipattern.description: Mixing framework idioms: @app.route in FastAPI, User.objects in FastAPI routes, Flask-Login in FastAPI projects.
antipattern.reason: Each framework has its own routing, ORM, and auth primitives. Mixing causes silent failures (Flask imports in FastAPI are resolved but behave differently) or runtime errors (User.objects requires django.setup()).
Deprecated in FastAPI 0.93+. Use the lifespan context manager instead.

antipattern.description: Quoting the unsourced RPS benchmark numbers (1,500/3,000+ RPS) as authoritative.
antipattern.reason: These numbers are context-dependent and not from a named benchmark. Always run a project-specific load test before citing performance claims.

antipattern.description: Starting with Flask for "simplicity" then adding Flask-RESTX, Flask-Migrate, Flask-Login, Flask-WTF until the dep list mirrors Django.
antipattern.reason: At that point Flask adds friction without adding features. The decision matrix should have chosen Django upfront.

antipattern.description: Using Flask async views expecting real concurrency under WSGI.
antipattern.reason: WSGI is synchronous. Async views run in a sync wrapper — no concurrency benefit, just added complexity.

rule.statement: Any change to settings.py / main.py / app.py (middleware order, security headers, CORS) requires human review — small edits have global effect.
rule.rationale: LLMs edit these files for convenience but middleware ordering bugs are notoriously hard to diagnose.

rule.statement: Never let an agent upgrade a framework version (Django 4 to 5, FastAPI 0.100 to 0.110) inside a feature task. That is its own audit task.
rule.rationale: Framework upgrades require testing all breaking changes; folding them into a feature task obscures regressions.
